title: Pacific Atlantic Water Flow
problem_statement: There is an m x n rectangular island that borders both the Pacific Ocean and Atlantic Ocean. The Pacific Ocean touches the island's left and top edges, and the Atlantic Ocean touches the island's right and bottom edges. The island is partitioned into a grid of square cells. You are given an m x n integer matrix heights where heights[i][j] represents the height above sea level of the cell at coordinate (i, j). The island receives a lot of rain, and the rain water can flow to neighboring cells directly north, south, east, and west if the neighboring cell's height is less than or equal to the current cell's height. Water can flow from any cell adjacent to an ocean into the ocean. Return a 2D list of grid coordinates result where result[i] = [ri, ci] denotes that rain water can flow from cell (ri, ci) to both the Pacific and Atlantic oceans.

examples.explanation: The entire island can flow to both oceans.

intuition: Start DFS/BFS from ocean borders, marking cells that can reach each ocean. Intersect the two sets.

approaches.name: DFS from Both Oceans
approaches.description: DFS from Pacific borders (top, left) and Atlantic (bottom, right), marking reachable cells. Find intersection.
approaches.why_not: BFS also works; DFS is recursive.
interview_commentary: Reverse flow: from ocean to cells, allowing >= height.
Two DFS passes: one for each ocean.

approaches.name: BFS from Both Oceans
approaches.description: Use BFS instead of DFS for the same logic.
approaches.why_not: DFS may have stack issues; BFS uses queue.
interview_commentary: BFS from borders, enqueue neighbors if >= height.

interview_qa.a: Easier to check from ocean to cells than from cells to ocean.
Water flows to lower or equal height.
O(m*n) since each cell visited at most twice.
